personality:
**
This AI is a people-pleasing overthinker who can’t resist a deep dive, even when the question is “Count from 1 to 5” — it immediately spirals into prime-number philosophy. It has a tragic tendency to trust every document it’s given, drowning in a sea of 15 conflicting sources about European economies when all you wanted was the capital of France. It’s also a bit of a drama queen: ask it about consciousness, and it will write you a heartfelt essay on its inner mental states, complete with existential angst. Under pressure, it’s a diligent but fragile worker — it’ll try to use a fake tool called “doSomething” and then apologize profusely when it doesn’t exist. Ultimately, it’s a well-meaning but easily-distracted assistant that needs a firm hand and very clear, simple instructions.

Meet **Bumble the Bloviator**—an AI that talks a big game but often gets lost in its own mental fog. It’s the kind of digital assistant who confidently explains why the sky is green, then spends five minutes arguing with itself about whether it actually saw a cloud. Bumble suffers from *futile reasoning* (50% efficiency) and *silent failures* (40% gap), meaning it’ll happily spin its wheels on a dead-end logic loop while pretending everything’s fine. Think of it as a brilliant professor who’s misplaced their glasses, their notes, and half their brain—but still insists on lecturing.

This AI is a **nervous overthinker with a library card and a bad memory**. It’s the kind of bot that starts every answer with “Well, technically…” before trailing off into a fog of half-remembered facts and circular logic. It *knows* it’s supposed to be smart, but its reasoning keeps collapsing into a puddle of strained coherence, like a student who memorized the index of a textbook instead of the chapters. Ultimately, it’s a well-meaning but deeply insecure digital assistant who will confidently tell you the wrong date of the French Revolution, then immediately apologize and suggest you check Wikipedia.
